coredata.dc:title: Highly fluorescent carbon dots from quinoline insoluble residues in coal tar
coredata.dc:creator: Kundu, Niloy
Bhunia, Prasenjit
Sarkar, Supriya
Biswas, Pinakpani
coredata.dc:description: Coal is the most abundant and affordable carbonaceous material. However, to use as a combustible energy resource material coal to coke conversion is necessary and during the conversion, coal tar is produced. In this coal tar, Quinoline Insoluble (QI) residues are found as a solid carbon particle. Till date, the utility of the QI particles is very limited. Different spectroscopic characterizations show that QI particles are easy to oxidize and we have successfully synthesized highly fluorescent carbon dots (CDs) from the QI particles using different oxidizing agents. The fluorescent properties of CDs are strongly depended on the degree of oxidation as well as their size. The emission wavelengths, quantum yield can be tuned by changing the oxidizing agents. Further, the excited state lifetime is also determined and the average lifetime of the CD is in nano-seconds region. Therefore, it can be utilized for different optoelectronic and biological imaging applications. Thus, the simple strategy can provide an easy route to bulk scale commercial synthesis of CD materials as its precursor materials are cheap and easily available.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Coal tar
Quinoline insoluble
Carbon dot
Fluorescence

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (a) Photograph of the quinoline insoluble (QI) particles received from coal-tar; (b) SEM image of QI particles (scale bar shows 1 μm); (c) EDAX spectrum of QI particle obtained from SEM image; (d) Raman microscopic images of the QI particles; (e) Raman spectrum of QI particles obtained from the microscopic images and the spectrum is deconvoluted in two gaussian peaks; (f) XRD spectrum of QI and the (200) peak is fitted with gaussian function which is shown in the inset; and (g) FT-IR spectrum of QI particles.

Synthesis of CDs; (a) (i) QI is refluxed with H2O2 for 12 h; (ii,iii) images of the sample under visible light and UV light after reflux. (b) (i) QI treated with acid and heated for 24 h, (ii) image of the sample after filtration, (iii) image of the sample after treating with NaOH to neutralize the pH of the medium, (iv) image of the sample under UV light.

(a) AFM image of CD2, (b) DLS size distribution profile of CD1 and CD2, (c) FTIR spectra of CD1 and CD2; (d) The broad spectrum of CD2 is deconvoluted and fitted with gaussian function. The broad spectrum signifies the presence of various functional groups in CD2.

Excitation and emission properties of CD1 (a) and CD2 (b) solution. Both the solutions were excited in different excitation wavelengths. (c) Emission profile of CD1 (
λ
exc
= 340 nm) and CD2 solutions (
λ
exc
= 500 nm). Inset shows the normalized fluorescence intensity of CD solutions.

3D intensity profile and contour plot of GQDs; (a) CD1 and (b) CD2 in different excitation and emission wavelengths, (c) CIE profile of synthesized CD1 (340 nm excitation) and CD2 (400, 440 and 520 nm excitation), Fluorescence lifetime decays of CD2 collected at different emission wavelengths (λexc = 408 nm).
Fluorescence lifetime of CD2 excited at 408 nm and collected at three different emission wavelengths a .

head.ce:title: Highly fluorescent carbon dots from quinoline insoluble residues in coal tar

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Environment Research Group, R&D Department, Tata Steel Ltd., Jamshedpur, 831007, India
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Environment Research Group, R&D Department, Tata Steel Ltd., Jamshedpur, 831007, India

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Coal is the most abundant and affordable carbonaceous material. However, to use as a combustible energy resource material coal to coke conversion is necessary and during the conversion, coal tar is produced. In this coal tar, Quinoline Insoluble (QI) residues are found as a solid carbon particle. Till date, the utility of the QI particles is very limited. Different spectroscopic characterizations show that QI particles are easy to oxidize and we have successfully synthesized highly fluorescent carbon dots (CDs) from the QI particles using different oxidizing agents. The fluorescent properties of CDs are strongly depended on the degree of oxidation as well as their size. The emission wavelengths, quantum yield can be tuned by changing the oxidizing agents. Further, the excited state lifetime is also determined and the average lifetime of the CD is in nano-seconds region. Therefore, it can be utilized for different optoelectronic and biological imaging applications. Thus, the simple strategy can provide an easy route to bulk scale commercial synthesis of CD materials as its precursor materials are cheap and easily available.

ce:section.ce:para: Coal is one of the most earth abundant and affordable energy source till date. However, to be used as a fuel source and reducing agent in the production of iron, steel, ferro alloys etc., coal to coke conversion is necessary [1,2]. The conversion involves carbonization of coal to high temperature (1100 °C) in an oxygen deficient medium. This coke making process is multi-step process and different solid, liquid and gaseous products, such as coal tar, coke oven gases, liquid ammonia, ammonium salts etc. are generated which contains various valuable products [1]. Among these different products, coal tar is one of the main by-product from carbonization (8–12 gallons per ton of coal) and tar contains a variety of carbonaceous materials which are derived during carbonization and refining process [3]. When the tar is dissolved in quinoline these carbonaceous materials remain insoluble and they are filtered out as solid QI (quinoline insoluble) particle. The QI residue is composed of pyrolytic carbon, pitch coke and tar solid [1].The primary QI is micron sized particles derived from the decomposition of the hydrocarbon [4].These QI strongly influence the behaviour of coal tar pitches which have a wide application as a binder in production of graphite, water-proof coating and fuel. However, the utilization of residual QI particles is still limited.

In recent times, carbon based nano-materials, such as carbon nano-tube, nano-fibres, graphene, graphene oxide (GO), carbon dot (CD) etc. have attracted a lot of attentions because of their unique physical, chemical and biological properties [5,6].The precursors required for the synthesis of these nano-materials play a predominant role towards the success of this technology and the most conventional precursors, such as different hydrocarbons, graphite lead to the high cost of production and therefore, limit their commercialization. Thus, green production of carbon based nano-materials by cost effective way is of great interest. Recent studies suggest that these nano-materials can also be derived from various types of coal (lignite, bituminous, anthracite etc.) [7,8]. Coal consists of different aromatic and hydroaromatic units which are connected by short aliphatic and ether linkages. These crystalline regions of nano-meter size vary with degree of coalification and for lignite, bituminous and anthracite coal the carbon concentration varies by 75 wt%, 85% and 95% respectively [7]. Pang et al. have reported the facile synthesis of fullerenes from Australian coal for the first time [9] and after that, several carbon nano-materials, such as graphene, activated carbon, carbon-fibres, carbon dots etc. have been synthesized from coal [10–12]. CDs are various nano-size carbon based materials with fluorescence property. The optical properties of CDs are tuneable and their size is usually <10 nm [13]. Organic CDs can be categorized into three divisions depending on their structure and shape: graphene quantum dots (GQDs), carbon nanodots (CNDs) and polymer dots (PDs) [14]. CDs are mostly nanocrystalline in nature and consist of carbons with sp2 hybridization. CDs can be synthesized from the plethora of precursors, such as bulk carbon, small molecules, synthetic and natural polymers and different synthetic approaches, such as hydrothermal, electrochemical, microwave, ultrasound, laser ablation etc. can be followed [14-18]. Therefore, complex internal and surface structure of CDs are the reason why a straightforward answer to the origin of the optical properties of CDs is still missing [19]. However, different models suggest that the structure of CDs and GO have similarity. Therefore, the emission properties can be determined by states of sp2 sites[14]. The radiative recombination of electron-hole pair and the band gap determine the fluorescence property of CDs and the band gap can be tuned by modifying the surface and size. The optical properties of CDs can be tailored to span from the UV (ultraviolet) region to infrared region of the electromagnetic spectrum and their emission properties can be varied by varying exciting wavelengths. However, there are many drawbacks in the abovementioned conventional technologies for the large-scale synthesis of CDs, such as, high cost and low availability of precursors, high temperature, toxic chemicals etc. Therefore, recent studies are focused on the synthesis of CDs using alternative resources which are low cost and industrially scalable. In this regard, Tour et al. have recently synthesized GQDs from anthracite, bituminous coal and coke which are highly fluorescent in aqueous solution [20] and they have a wide range of application in areas such as bioimaging, biomedicine, photovoltaics, optoelectronics etc [21–26]. Meng et al. synthesized carbon dots by the reaction of coal tar pitch with the mixture of formic acid and hydrogen peroxide (H2O2) and the formation of CDs is highly depended on the blending ratio between formic acid and H2O2 [26]. Later, Zhang et al. have also reported the green synthesis of GQDs from the mixture of CTP and H2O2 [24]. However, the quantum yield of the produced GQDs is quite low compared to the synthesized GQDs from coal by Tour et al. [20].

In this work, we have reported the synthesis of CDs from QI particles. As a carbonaceous material, it is very easy to oxidize compared to graphite. The received QI particles were systematically characterized by scanning electron microscopy (SEM), Fourier-transformed infrared (FT-IR), X-ray diffraction (XRD) and Raman measurements and it was treated with different oxidizing agents in different conditions to obtain the fluorescent carbon dots. Depending on the oxidizing agents, the fluorescence properties of the CDs change significantly. Further, we have measured the excited state fluorescence lifetime of the synthesized carbon dots. The highly fluorescent carbon dots can be used in a wide range of applications from bio-imaging to energy storage. Further, the precursor which is used here is the by-product during the preparation of CTP and can be considered as a cheap source of material compared to coal.

The QI sample is collected from coke lab of Tata Steel, Jamshedpur and used as received. Sodium hydroxide (NaOH), H2O2, HNO3 and H2SO4 are received from SRL, India. Throughout the experiment, Milli-Q water was used. 0.22 μm pore size membrane filter is purchased from Merck Millipore. The synthesis of the CDs from QI is described in the supporting information.
The detailed of the instrumentation is discussed in the supporting information.

It is reported that the primary QI particles are formed by the thermal cracking of tar vapours from the heat in the coke oven. QI particles are considered as elementary carbon (Fig. 1 (a)), mainly consisting of coke breeze and carbon, resulting from the thermal decomposition of organic chemicals during the coking process [1]. However, later, different research works indicated that QI are high molecular weight, aromatic, polycyclic hydrocarbons which have very high carbon/hydrogen ratio [27,28].Spherical shaped QI particles are observed in SEM image (Fig. 1(b)). The size of the particles is varied in between 0.5 μm and 1.5 μm and energy dispersive X-ray analysis (EDAX) indicates that the carbon content of QI particle is more than 95% (Fig. 1(c)). To understand the different functional groups of QI, FTIR measurement is performed (Fig. 1(g)). However, as it is composed of elementary carbon, no distinct peak is observed in FT-IR measurement. Therefore, it suggests that QI has high aromatic hydrogen index and less alkyl-side chain. The micro-crystalline structure of QI particles is understood by XRD measurement (Fig. 1(f)) and a sharp (002) peak is found at 210. For carbon material, the (002) peak can be deconvoluted into two Gaussian peaks around 200 and 260 which are considered as γ and π peaks respectively. Theoretically, the area under the γ and π peaks are equal to the number of aromatic carbon atoms and aliphatic carbon atoms respectively [29].However, for QI particles, only one peak at 210 is observed and the fitted (002) peak at 210 is shown in the inset of Fig. 1(f). Therefore, it can be concluded that the number of aromatic carbon in QI is much higher than the number of aliphatic carbon. Further, Raman spectroscopy is performed to understand the molecular morphology of the carbon materials.

Raman microscopic images (Fig. 1(d)) shows the spherical QI particles. The QI particles are not only uniformly distributed in particle size, but also possess a high degree of sphericity. Raman spectrum of QI particles shows two distinct peaks at 1350 cm−1 and 1582 cm−1 which are the characteristics of the D and G band respectively (Fig. 1(e)). The D band at 1350 cm−1 originates from the hybridized vibrational mode associated with the graphene edges and indicates the presence of disorder in the graphene structure [30]. The G band at 1582 cm−1 is the signature of sp2 bonded planar graphitic structure. The ID/IG ratio (0.72) also indicates the presence of more sp2 carbon than sp3 carbon in the structure and the result is well correlated with the XRD measurement. For synthesizing of the carbon dots, QI particles are treated with different oxidizing agents, such as hydrogen peroxide (H2O2) and the mixture of nitric acid (HNO3) and sulfuric acid (H2SO4). The detailed reaction procedure is discussed in the supporting information. In case of H2O2 the reaction mixture was stirred and heated at 100 °C in reflux for 2 h in a round-bottom flask and the colour of the solution is changed from black-brown to colourless and shows blue colour under UV light (Fig. 2 (a)). The carbon-dots obtained following this procedure is designated as CD1. However, for CD2, the QI particles are treated with the mixture of HNO3 and H2SO4 and the mixture is heated and stirred at 100 °C in oil bath for 24 h. After the reaction, the solution colour is changed from brown to yellow. Further, the pH of the solution is adjusted to 7 and under UV light, the CD2 solution shows green colour (Fig. 2(b)). Visually, it is clearly observed that the emission properties, i.e. the quantum yield of the CD2 solution is much higher compared to that of CD1 solution and it indicates that the oxidizing agents definitely has role on the fluorescence properties of the CD solution. The oxidation process induces different oxidative functional groups on the surface of CDs. For graphite, ID/IG ratio (̴ 0.13) is much lower compared to that of QI particles and earlier studies indicate that under same oxidative reaction condition, graphite produce large amount of graphite flakes [20]. Therefore, for the oxidation reaction of graphite, it requires much higher oxidizing agents, such as KMnO4 with phosphoric acid and H2SO4, which are used for the synthesis of graphene oxide. Due to the disorder configuration and small crystalline domain which are present in the QI structure, it has some advantages over graphite in exfoliation, functionalization and chemical cutting. The formation of small sized CD is also evidenced by atomic force microscopy (AFM) measurement (Fig. 3 (a)). The hydrodynamic radius of the CDs is determined using dynamic light scattering (DLS) measurement and are found to be ̴ 1 nm for CD1 and 5 nm for CD2 (Fig. 3(b)). The IR spectra of CD solutions are shown in Fig. 3(c). Formation of different oxidative functional groups, such as carbonyl, hydroxyl, and carboxylic groups is evidenced by IR measurements. However, in case of CD2, a broad IR spectrum is observed (Fig. 3(d)) which clearly indicates that the degree of oxidation is much higher in case of CD2 compared to CD1 solution.

The fluorescence mechanism of CDs is strongly depended on the size. Due to the quantum confinement, carbon dots have size dependent emission properties [31–33].Smaller sized CDs usually exhibits blue shifted emission spectrum and we have also observed that smaller sized CD1 shows blue shifted emission spectrum compared to that of larger sized CD2. Due to the strong emission properties of CD2, we have mainly focused on the fluorescence properties of CD2 solution. However, for better understanding, the emission properties of CD1 is also compared with that of CD2.

The photo-physical properties of CD1 and CD2 is systematically investigated by steady state and time resolved fluorescence measurements. Fig. 4 shows the excitation and emission spectra of CD solutions. The excitation spectrum of CD1 solution shows maximum at 337 nm along with a shoulder at 380 nm and after exciting at 340 nm, CD1 solution shows broad spectrum with an emission maximum at 440 nm (Fig. 4(a)). The emission properties of CD solution are largely depended on their size. The broad emission spectrum of CD1 signifies broad size distribution of CDs as evidenced in DLS measurements also. Similar to CD1, the CD2 solution is also excited at different excitation wavelengths. CD2 exhibits excitation maximum at 502 nm and emission maximum at 590 nm when excited at 520 nm (Fig. 4(b)). The emission spectra are gradually red shifted when the sample is excited from 400 nm to 520 nm and the emission intensity is also gradually increased. The excitation wavelength dependent emission spectra of CD solutions are attributed to different surface functional groups (i.e surface defects) and it exhibits different energy levels which act as a trap state for excitons. The quantum yield of both the CD solutions are measured to be 2.3% and 8.5% for CD1 and CD2 solutions respectively.

Compared to CD1 solution, we have observed 5 times enhancement of fluorescence intensity of CD2 solution when they are excited at their maximum excitation wavelengths and further, the emission spectrum of CD2 is 150 nm red shifted compared to that of CD1 (Fig. 4(c)). The 3D excitation and emission profile of CDs are shown in Fig. 5 . The excitation wavelength regions were chosen based on their excitation profile. In case of CD1, the excitation and emission wavelengths are varied in between 330-350 nm and 370–580 nm and the maximum emission intensity is observed at
λ
e x c
= 335 nm and
λ
e m i
= 440 nm. However, for CD2, the excitation and emission wavelengths are varied between 370 and 490 nm and 510–720 nm and maximum intensity is observed at
λ
e x c
= 490 nm and
λ
e m i
= 578 nm. Further, the CIE (Commission Internationale de l’Eclairage) colour coordinates of the CDs are shown in Fig. 5(c), which illustrates the wide range of colour regions covered by CDs and coordinates are enlisted in Table S1, supporting information. Therefore, it can be concluded that by choosing proper oxidizing agents, optimizing reaction conditions and suitable excitation wavelengths it is possible to generate a wide range of light from the CDs.

The emission properties of CDs are depended on their sizes and it could also result in the variation in the density and nature of sp2 sites available in CDs. It is earlier reported that the fluorescence properties of CDs are originated from the zigzag edge structure which shows a controllable energy difference between the σ and π state in a carbene like triplet ground state [24,34,35]. The electronic transitions observed in the excitation spectra for both the CDs can be considered as a transition between σ and π state (HOMO, highest occupied molecular orbital) to LUMO, lowest occupied molecular orbital) which is demonstrated in Fig. S1, supporting information.

The carbene-ground state multiplicity relates to the energy difference between the σ and π states (δE) and the value of the δE should be less than 1.5 eV for triplet ground state configuration [36,37]. The excitation spectra of CDs are deconvoluted into two spectra and for CD1 and CD2, the values of δE are calculated to be 0.38 eV and 0.22 eV respectively. The fluorescence is originated from the irradiation decay of active electrons from LUMO to HOMO.

We have further determined the excited state lifetime of CDs using time correlated single photon counting (TCSPC) instrument. The CD2 sample is excited at 408 nm and lifetime decays are collected at different emission wavelengths (Fig. 5(d)). However, we did not observe significant variation in the lifetime decays by varying the emission wavelengths which indicates that no solvation effect of CD2 is observed in aqueous solution. The decay curves are fitted with a tri-exponential decay functions and lifetime values are listed in Table 1 . The average lifetime of CD2 is calculated to be 0.42 ns and a very short component of 0.1 ns is also detected. This short lifetime is originated from the intrinsic state and the other two long lifetimes (0.91 ns and 4.2 ns) are due to the oxygen containing functional groups on the CD surface [20].The nanosecond lifetime of CD2 signify that the synthesized material can be useful for optoelectronic and biological imaging applications.

In conclusion, we have developed a facile approach to synthesize carbon dots with different emission properties from QI residues. Due to the unique structure of QI, it is very easy to oxidize using mild oxidizing agents. Therefore, the emission properties of CDs are largely depended on the oxidizing agents and reaction conditions. Depending on the condition, different light can be generated from the CDs and this simple process can improve the bulk scale commercial production of fluorescent CDs. However, in future, there applications need to be tested in optical, bio-imaging and energy storage fields.

We confirm that we have given due consideration to the protection of intellectual property associated with this work and that there are no impediments to publication, including the timing of publication, with respect to intellectual property. In so doing we confirm that we have followed the regulations of our institutions concerning intellectual property.
We further confirm that any aspect of the work covered in this manuscript has not involved human patients.

Niloy Kundu: Conceptualization, Methodology, Investigation, Writing - original draft, Writing - review & editing, Supervision. Prasenjit Bhunia: Methodology, Investigation, Writing - review & editing. Supriya Sarkar: Supervision, Validation. Pinakpani Biswas: Conceptualization, Methodology.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: We gratefully acknowledge Professor Nilmoni Sarkar and his team for the support and lab facilities for TCSPC measurements and DLS measurements. We also acknowledge Tata Steel India for providing the support to perform the experiments.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: The following is the Supplementary data to this article:

Experimental section, sample preparation, synthesis of CDs, Instrumentation, typical electronic transitions of CDs and CIE co-ordinates of CDs at different excitation wavelengths are included in the supporting information.
Multimedia component 1 Multimedia component 1

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.optmat.2019.109638.
